learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells: Future Efficiency Leaders
learn.intro: Perovskite solar cells promise to revolutionize renewable energy with higher efficiency and lower costs than silicon. Their unique properties enable rapid advancements, making them a key player in the global shift toward clean energy.
learn.c1_text: Perovskite cells convert sunlight into electricity at over 25% efficiency, surpassing traditional silicon cells. Their ability to absorb light across a broader spectrum allows for more energy generation in smaller spaces.
learn.c2_text: Unlike silicon, perovskite materials can be printed using inkjet technology, drastically reducing production costs. This could lower solar panel prices by up to 30%, making renewable energy more accessible.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar capacity is projected to reach 500 GW by 2030. Perovskite technology could help meet this target by enabling rooftop installations in urban areas with limited space.
learn.stat2: Perovskite cells achieved 26% efficiency in lab tests (2023)
learn.stat3: Perovskite production costs could be 20% lower than silicon by 2030
learn.stat4: India’s solar manufacturing sector employs over 100,000 people (2023)

learn.title: Wind Turbine Lifespan and Blade Recycling
learn.intro: Wind turbines last 20–25 years, but blade disposal poses environmental challenges. Innovations in recycling and repurposing are critical to sustaining wind energy’s role in the clean energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Modern wind turbines operate for 20–25 years, but regular maintenance is essential. A single blade can weigh 10 tons, requiring specialized equipment for repairs and inspections.
learn.c2_text: Only 5% of wind turbine blades are recycled globally. New methods like pyrolysis and chemical breakdown are being tested to convert blades into materials for construction or energy production.
learn.c3_text: India’s wind energy sector is exploring partnerships to recycle blades into composites for road construction. The country aims to recycle 100% of blades by 2030 under its circular economy policies.
learn.stat3: India’s wind energy sector employs 50,000+ workers (2023)

learn.title: Green Ammonia: Clean Fuel and Fertilizer
learn.intro: Green ammonia is a zero-emission alternative for shipping and agriculture. Produced using renewable energy, it offers a sustainable solution for decarbonizing industries reliant on fossil fuels.
learn.c1_text: Green ammonia is made via electrolysis powered by renewables, eliminating CO2 emissions. It stores 12 kg of hydrogen per ton, making it ideal for long-term energy storage.
learn.c2_text: Ammonia powers ships and aircraft with no emissions. It also replaces synthetic fertilizers, reducing agricultural emissions by up to 30% while boosting crop yields.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Green Hydrogen Mission targets 5 million tons of green ammonia by 2030. Policies like subsidies for renewable energy production support this transition.
learn.stat1: India: 5 million tons green ammonia target (2030)
learn.stat4: Green ammonia reduces fertilizer emissions by 30% (IRENA)

learn.title: Flow Batteries for Grid Storage
learn.intro: Flow batteries store energy in liquid electrolytes, offering scalable, long-duration storage. Vanadium and iron systems are leading solutions for stabilizing renewable energy grids.
learn.c1_text: Flow batteries decouple power and energy capacity, allowing independent scaling. A 10 MWh system can store energy for 10 hours, ideal for grid stability.
learn.c2_text: Vanadium flow batteries use the same electrolyte for both sides, reducing costs. Iron-based systems are cheaper but require more complex chemistry, making them suitable for large-scale projects.
learn.c3_text: India’s grid storage capacity is expected to grow to 10 GW by 2030. Projects like the Andhra Pradesh solar-wind hybrid plant use flow batteries to balance supply and demand.

learn.title: EV Charging Infrastructure: Level 1, 2, and DC
learn.intro: EV charging levels vary in speed and cost. Understanding these differences helps users choose the right infrastructure for homes, workplaces, and highways.
learn.c1_text: Level 1 charging uses a standard 120V outlet, adding 3–5 miles of range per hour. It’s ideal for overnight charging at home but not for rapid top-ups.
learn.c2_text: Level 2 chargers (240V) deliver 10–25 miles of range per hour. They’re common in public stations and workplaces, offering a balance between speed and cost.
learn.c3_text: DC fast chargers (400–1,000V) provide 60–200 miles of range in 15–30 minutes. They’re essential for highways but require high-power infrastructure.

learn.title: Small Wind Turbines for Homes and Communities
learn.intro: Small wind turbines generate clean energy for homes and communities. They’re ideal for remote areas and can reduce reliance on the grid while lowering electricity bills.
learn.c1_text: Small turbines (under 100 kW) use blades to capture wind energy, converting it into electricity via a generator. They’re designed for low-wind areas, often with vertical or horizontal axis designs.
learn.c2_text: They power homes, farms, and rural communities. A 10 kW turbine can supply 10–15% of a household’s annual energy needs, reducing grid dependency.
learn.c3_text: India’s small wind sector is expanding, with projects in Tamil Nadu and Rajasthan. The government offers subsidies for rural installations, aiming to add 500 MW by 2025.
learn.stat4: Small turbines reduce grid reliance by 20–30% (2023 data)
